title-group.article-title: Emerging heat stress patterns across India under future climate scenarios

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/01c27hj86grid.9983.b0000 0001 2181 4263IDL - Instituto Dom Luiz, Faculdade de Ciências, Universidade de Lisboa, 1749-016 Lisbon, Portugal
2https://ror.org/00582g326grid.19003.3b0000 0000 9429 752XDepartment of Hydrology, Indian Institute of Technology, Roorkee, 247667 India
3https://ror.org/04z8jg394grid.23731.340000 0000 9195 2461 Section 4.4: Hydrology, GFZ German Research Centre for Geosciences, 14473 Postdam, Germany
4https://ror.org/03490as77grid.8536.80000 0001 2294 473X Departamento de Meteorologia, Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

abstract.p: This study investigates the climate change signal on mean and extreme temperatures in India, adding new insight by using the state-of-the-art CMIP6 projections to quantify the seasonal and spatial evolution of the Heat Index (HI) across India.; offering one of the first national-scale assessments combining temperature and humidity under Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) scenarios. To assess the recent past climate change signal on those properties, ERA5 reanalysis data are used. CMIP6 models realistically reproduce historical warming patterns during winter (DJF) and pre-monsoon (MAM) seasons but tend to underestimate extreme summer (JJA) conditions. Future daily HI is calculated from maximum temperature and relative humidity from CMIP6 global climate models (GCMs). Future projections indicate a substantial increase in both the frequency and persistence of dangerous HI levels across India, driven by rising temperatures and regionally variable humidity trends. By mid-21st century (2041–2070), the annual number of days with dangerously high HI values (27º and 32 °C) is projected to rise by more than 50 and 5 days, respectively, compared to 1971–2000. By the late century (2071–2100) under the SSP5-8.5 scenario, the HI will be above 27 °C (32 °C) during more than 75 (75) absolute days per year in JJA and more than 75 (20) days in MAM. Critical HI days will be highest in coastal regions in winter and more northern regions in summer, increasing towards northern latitudes with the emission scenario. These findings underscore the importance of region-specific adaptation strategies, as heat stress future anomalies will differ across India. Understanding these spatiotemporal patterns is critical for effective climate adaptation and public health policies aimed at mitigating the increasing risks associated with extreme heat events.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s41598-026-36299-3.

institution-wrap.institution: Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia
Instituto Dom Luiz, Universidade de Lisboa

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Global warming has accelerated markedly over the past four decades, pushing the planetary mean temperature ~ 1.2 °C above its pre-industrial level and driving a disproportionate rise in the frequency, intensity and duration of heat extremes worldwide1–3. The impact of extreme heat in humans and ecosystems has been comprehensively studied in Europe4,5 and North America6,7, however that is not the case for the Indian subcontinent. India is emerging as a global hot-spot: the 2015 heatwave caused more than 3500 certified deaths, and health-registry analyses indicate that national heatwave mortality climbed by about 55% between the early 2000s and the 2017–2021 period8. Recent probabilistic projections suggest that, without effective adaptation, extreme heat could claim up to 1.5 million additional Indian lives every year by 21009. These trends underscore the urgency of understanding how heat stress will evolve in a warming, humidifying climate10.

Extreme heat refers to hotter and/or more humid conditions than normal in a location6,11. Therefore, defining extreme heat depends on the location being studied, since the response of the human body is determined by the climate to which it is adapted. To study the effect of ambient conditions on health, indices based on percentiles of air temperature, humidity, wind speed, or solar and thermal radiation are commonly used12,13. Previous studies show that the effects of temperature extremes on heat stress14–16 are accentuated by high humidity, low wind, radiation loads, and metabolic activity17–19. Relative humidity is relevant for physiological stress because it describes the ability to lose heat through evaporative cooling20–22. In this context, a suite of biometeorological indices—Wet-Bulb Temperature, Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature, the Universal Thermal Climate Index and the Heat Index (HI)—were proposed to translate these meteorological drivers into physiologically meaningful metrics23.

The heat index (HI) is used here to quantify the impact of climate change on human health. Despite the availability of multiple indices to estimate heat exposure24,25, most algorithms have been found to generate values consistent with the HI24. HI, originally developed by NOAA and used operationally by the U.S. National Weather Service, has proven to be a useful tool for assessing heat stress by integrating the combined effect of temperature and moisture on physiological stress, assuming constant/normal levels of vapour pressure, wind speed and extra radiation22,26. The HI considers the physiological capacity for thermoregulation through sweat evaporation and blood flow to the skin, which makes it relevant to evaluate heat stress in extremely hot conditions26; however, its applicability varies according to the regional climatic and socio-economic context27. Humidity is critical: high vapour pressure throttles evaporative cooling, so heat stress in India often peaks not in the hottest pre-monsoon afternoons but during moist monsoon nights along the eastern seaboard20,21.

Observational records show that India warmed by about 0.64 °C century⁻¹ over 1901–2016, with the steepest rise after 1980 attributed to intensified greenhouse gas forcing, rapid land-use changes, and circulation shifts that have coincided with reduced low cloud cover and weakened moisture advection from the Arabian Sea28,29.

Heatwave days have increased most sharply in the north-west and Indo-Gangetic Plain30, while humid-heat stress is intensifying along both coasts31. From a seasonal perspective, the pre-monsoon (March through May) is the warmest of the year, associated with significant thermal extreme impacts on urban populations and public health systems32–34. CMIP6 model ensembles project that, under a high-emissions pathway, the country could see a nationwide increase of 7–17 heatwave days by 2080 relative to the recent past3,35–38 projected an increase in nighttime heat events larger than daytime events, and a 4-fold rise in heatwave frequency due to the increase of 1.5 °C (RCP8.5) in global mean temperature for 2070–2090. This poses a major challenge for human health and prevention policy planning, as risk exposure to extreme conditions depends on the combination of the geographical distribution of populations with the number of critical days of extreme heat in each location39. Yet three important knowledge gaps remain. First, the role played by humidity is often ignored: most Indian studies evaluate dry-bulb temperature or heatwave magnitude indices that omit moisture, despite evidence that humid heat can reach physiologically critical levels even at moderate temperatures. Second, seasonal asymmetry is poorly characterized: research has focused on the March–May pre-monsoon period, leaving winter (DJF) and monsoon (JJA) heat stress—when maritime relative humidity is highest—largely unexplored. Third, duration metrics are scarce: consecutive-day exceedances of HI thresholds, which strongly control health outcomes, have seldom been quantified for India. At the same time, modelling uncertainties complicate HI estimates. CMIP6 global climate models (GCMs) show skill in reproducing India’s mean seasonal temperatures, but they display warm–dry biases in summer and wet biases in winter30, and their representation of compound temperature–humidity extremes has only recently begun to be evaluated.

This study addresses these gaps by (i) evaluating CMIP6 model skill against ERA5 reanalysis for maximum temperature across winter, pre-monsoon and monsoon seasons; (ii) quantifying future changes not only in the frequency but also in the persistence of HI exceedances (≥ 27 °C and ≥ 32 °C) under SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios for the 2041–2070 and 2071–2100 periods; and (iii) identifying emerging regional hot-spots—including wintertime coastal stress and summertime northern maxima—. By providing the first nationwide, season-resolved HI projections based on state-of-the-art CMIP6 data, the work delivers actionable insights for heat-action plans, urban planning and occupational-safety standards in one of the world’s most climate-vulnerable nations.

sec.title: Reanalysis data: ERA5
sec.p: To analyse present climate trends and variability of temperature over India, ERA5 daily surface air temperature for the 1951–2020 period are used. ERA5 is the fifth-generation reanalysis developed at the ECMWF (C3S, DOI:10.24381/cds.adbb2d47), provides hourly estimates for a large amount of atmospheric, ocean wave and land surface variables40. The atmospheric component is interpolated to 137 pressure levels from the surface up to 1 Pa. It has a horizontal grid resolution of 0.25 degrees, corresponding to around 31 km. ERA5 is freely available through the EU-funded Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S, https://climate.copernicus.eu/) from 1950 to the present. More information about ERA5 characteristics can be found in41.

sec.title: CMIP6 global climate models
sec.p: Daily temperature data from a 15 Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP642, GCMs ensemble (available) were obtained from the Earth Grid Federation (ESGF, https://esgf-node.llnl.gov/search/cmip6). A model representative of each participant Institution with available data for the historical (1951–2014), SSP1-26, SSP2-45 and SSP5-85 experiments (2015–2100) was chosen (see Table S1). They have different horizontal resolutions, often larger than 1.25° in latitude and longitude. Thus, for producing spatial analyses, all ERA5 and CMIP6 data were regridded to a common 1.25 × 1.25° lat/lon regular grid using conservative remapping as in3 before starting the analysis. An adiabatic temperature correction was performed to ensure that all comparisons are computed at the same height, i.e., before the model interpolation, temperatures are adjusted to sea level with a constant lapse rate of 6.5 °C/km and, afterwards, they are again corrected to the target grid orography assuming the same lapse rate43.

sec.title: Observations data
sec.p: The IMD High resolution daily gridded maximum temperature data set developed by44 for the Indian region was used as reference for the 1951 to 2020 period. It is based on quality-controlled observations from 284 meteorological stations, interpolated onto a 1° latitude × 1° longitude grid using a modified Shepard’s angular distance weighting algorithm. Cross-validation shows interpolation errors are typically less than 0.5 °C, and the dataset is comparable in quality to other high-resolution temperature products45. This dataset is widely used for climate variability, trend analysis, and extreme event studies in India due to its high spatial and temporal resolution and robust quality control.

sec.title: Climate change signal in temperatures
sec.p: The spatiotemporal variability of the seasonal climate, at the regional scale, is studied by analysing the standardised decadal frequency distributions of monthly surface air temperature for the historical (1951–2014) and the future (2015–2100) periods. Following the methodology proposed by3,46, the shift in seasonal mean temperature is expressed in standard deviation (SD) units to emphasize the anomalous signal-to-noise ratio of seasonal temperature tails. For each decade or analysed period, seasonal anomalies in SD units are computed at each grid point. These values are subsequently used to construct density curves. This approach allows identifying if the warming signal emerges beyond the internal variability of the respective region.

The “shift” metric quantifies the deviation of the seasonal mean temperature for a given decade from the climatological baseline (1951–1980), expressed in standard deviation units. Since extreme heat is characterized by temperatures and humidity levels exceeding normal conditions, this study also examines values exceeding 3σ, which are classified as extreme anomalies.

sec.title: Heat-index
sec.p: The simple HI is computed first. If the resulting HI is 26.6 °C or higher, the full adjustments described above are applied.

To evaluate the frequency of heat stress events with relevance to health and to quantify changes in the duration of extreme heat events each season, the number of events above 27 °C (HI27+) and 32 °C (HI32+), and the longest consecutive number of days with HI27 + and HI32 + conditions, are calculated for each year (92 days) and model. The number of HI27 + days includes all days above the HI32 + threshold. These indices can lead to fatigue possible with prolonged exposure and/or physical activity (HI27+) or heat stroke, heat cramps, or heat exhaustion with prolonged exposure and/or physical activity (32+), and refer to the Caution and Extreme caution levels, respectively, used by the National Weather Service Forecast Office (NWS) general heat advisory guidance6,55. All calculations and figures in this work were created with Python Software Foundation. Python Language Reference, version 2.7. Available at http://www.python.org, using Matplotlib56.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: This section presents a detailed analysis of historical and future seasonal temperature anomalies and heat stress conditions across India, using initially ERA5 reanalysis and afterwards the CMIP6 projections.

sec.title: Seasonal evolution of temperature anomalies in historical observations and CMIP6 projections
sec.p: This section analyses the temporal evolution and significance of climate change signals in seasonal temperature extremes across India, quantified through standardized decadal frequency distributions of monthly maximum temperature anomalies.

Figure 1 displays the standardized decadal maximum temperature anomaly distributions derived from the IMD gridded observations and the ERA5 reanalysis for the historical period (1951–2020) with reference to 1951–1980, and the projected CMIP6 model outputs for future periods (2015–2100). In Fig. 1a, the standardized decadal temperature anomaly distribution between 1951 and 2020 for DJF (first column), MAM (second column) and JJA (third column) seasons is presented. For the historical period, a clear shift in the anomaly distribution toward higher temperatures (positive anomalies) is evident, beginning notably around 1990, as seen for the monthly mean temperatures in3. The frequency of extreme hot anomalies (> 3σ) has increased notably in DJF, rising by approximately 1.3% during the last two decades compared to the 1951–1980 baseline. This pronounced winter warming aligns with observed global circulation changes, including weakening Northern Hemisphere jet streams and reductions in cold-air advection events during winter months57,58. Additionally, localized feedback mechanisms, such as reduced snow covers and diminished Himalayan snowpack, have contributed to increased surface heating in northern India during winter59,60.

Seasonal variations are also clearly visible in ERA5 results. During the pre-monsoon season (MAM), temperature distributions show enhanced bimodality, featuring increased frequency around both colder (− 1σ) and warmer anomalies (up to + 2σ). This bimodal behaviour likely relates to heightened intra-seasonal variability in India’s pre-monsoon months, influenced by alternating dry and wet periods, variable moisture advection from surrounding oceans, and early onset of localized convective activity61. In contrast, monsoon (JJA) temperatures reveal reduced occurrences of cold anomalies and increased frequencies around moderately warm anomalies (1.5–2σ). The reduction in cold anomalies during monsoon season in recent decades is likely associated with reduced rainfall frequency and intensification of prolonged dry spells under a warming climate, consistent with observed weakening of the Indian Summer Monsoon circulation over recent decades62,63.

To evaluate how accurately current state-of-the-art GCMs reproduce the climate change signal in India, the historical temperature distributions were also calculated for the climatic periods 1951–1980 and 1985–2014, using the former as a reference. For such analysis, the CMIP6 GCM ensemble was compared with the ERA5 reanalysis dataset and the gridded observations, as illustrated in Fig. 1b. Although the observations are unable to capture the bimodal distribution during MAM and JJA due to the limited number of meteorological stations in the Himalayan region, where mostly of the negative anomalies are produced, they nonetheless provide a realistic representation of the regional climate conditions. Our results show that, in general, the ensemble mean of the CMIP6 models can represent the seasonal distributions of standardized temperature anomalies as represented by ERA5 reanalysis. Notably, CMIP6 captures the winter warming signal (DJF) quite accurately, although slightly overestimating the occurrence of extremely hot events (by 0.33% in reference to ERA5 and by 0.24% to observations). Similarly, the ensemble overestimates extreme hot anomalies and shift magnitudes in MAM (by approximately 0.1σ with respect to the observations) and JJA (by approximately 0.1σ shift and 0.10% of extreme events). These biases in CMIP6 simulations align well with previously reported limitations of coarse-resolution GCMs in representing localized and seasonal extremes across the Indian subcontinent, often linked to under-resolved convection processes, coarse representation of land-atmosphere feedbacks, and biases in moisture transport from oceans3,63.

Future projections based on CMIP6 models under three SSP scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5) exhibit pronounced shifts toward higher temperature anomalies, along with significantly flattened distributions, indicating amplified extremes and decreased occurrences of historically “normal” temperatures. Under the low-emission scenario (SSP1-2.6, Fig. 1c), the shift reaches 0.7σ in DJF, 0.6σ in MAM, and 0.5σ in JJA, by the end of the century, with respective extreme anomaly (> 3σ) frequencies of approximately 2.1%, 0.8%, and 2.2%. Physically, this relatively greater increase during winter could reflect strong land-surface feedback and reductions in wintertime snow cover, combined with global-scale warming that is more pronounced during colder months51.

In the intermediate scenario (SSP2-4.5, Fig. 1d), mid-century anomalies resemble those of SSP1-2.6, by the end of the century, highlighting that even moderate emission pathways will lead to substantial warming. Under the high-emission scenario (SSP5-8.5, Fig. 1e), warming becomes rather pronounced, especially in DJF, with a 2.3σ shift by the late 21st century and 28.1% of monthly anomalies exceeding the extreme (3σ) threshold. The substantial projected warming in DJF may reflect intensified polar amplification processes, weakened Northern Hemisphere circulation, and significant reductions in cold-air outbreaks affecting northern India58,64. Although warming in the pre-monsoon season (MAM) is lower (1.6σ), a significant reduction in cold anomalies indicates enhanced temperature variability and the dominance of warm extremes. During the monsoon season (JJA), projections indicate both reductions in cold anomalies and pronounced increases in extreme hot anomalies (over 17.7% events exceeding 3σ), corresponding to intensifying monsoon breaks and drying trends observed in the recent historical period63,65.

caption.p: Frequency of seasonal temperature anomalies (DJF – first column, MAM – second column, JJA – third column), expressed as deviations from the local 1951–1980 mean and normalized by the local standard deviation, obtained by counting grid boxes with anomalies in each 0.05 interval. The area under each curve is unity. The shift is shown at the left of the curve, and the percentage of events above 3σ is at the right. For (a) the recent past from ERA5 reanalysis at decadal scale, (b) the CMIP6 GCMs ensemble mean (continuous line) compared to ERA5 (discontinuous line) and observations (dashdot line) in the 1985–2014 historical period, (c) SSP1-2.6, (d) SSP2-4.5 and (e) SSP5-8.5 future scenarios for the 2015–2040, 2041–2070, and 2071–2100 periods.

sec.title: Regional and seasonal variability of heat index (HI) under future climate scenarios
sec.p: The heat index (HI) is a metric that quantifies the perceived temperature resulting from the combined effects of air temperature and relative humidity. This measure indicates thermal sensation, as humidity significantly impacts the body’s ability to regulate heat through the evaporation of sweat26. The HI is determined by temperature and modulated by relative humidity, thus, changes in these variables might impact human health.

Figure 2 shows the ensemble mean projection of HI for the mid (2041–2070, first row) and far (2071–2100, second row) future periods, under the low-emission SSP1-2.6 scenario. The results show regionally distinct patterns. Although the 100 km resolution is limited to capture coastal circulations66, the highest HI values (27–32 °C) are found on the Southern Coast in DJF and in the northern half of the country in JJA, which suppose an increment to “Caution” levels in reference to the historical HI seen in Fig. S4. These spatial patterns result from a complex interplay between temperature and humidity dynamics (see Supplementary Fig. S1) associated with the Indian Ocean’s moisture transport pathways, notably during pre-monsoon and monsoon seasons67. HI stabilizes over time, corresponding with the temperature equilibrium projected under low-emission SSP1-2.6 scenario. In the mid-century, the climatological distribution shows a generalized HI lower than 27 °C from December to May, and a negative HI in the northern Himalayan region, related to a future decrease in relative humidity in this area (Fig. S1). In JJA, the HI is projected to reach the Caution levels (> 27 °C) in most of the country, reaching an HI > 32 °C in the Northern regions. This is due to the higher increase of temperature projected in the Northern region (see Supplementary Fig. S1). Looking at the spatial distribution of future temperature and relative humidity anomalies (Fig. S1), it is seen that the increases in temperature in the SSP1-2.6 scenario will be surpassed by an increment in relative humidity greater than 2% in the mid-century over the Northwest, and it will be doubled in the late-century. Decreases in relative humidity of -2% will occur in the Northeast and of -1% in Southern India during MAM.

caption.p: Heat-Index mean projections for the SSP1-2.6 scenario for the middle (2041–2070; top row) and far (2071–2100; bottom row) futures. Created with Matplotlib in Python Language Reference, version 2.7. Available at http://www.python.org.

sec.p: Under the intermediate emission scenario SSP2-4.5 (Fig. 3), the mean HI spatial patterns resemble SSP1-2.6, yet with modest intensification of warming signals. Notably, although temperature anomalies increase significantly (Fig. S2, mid-century: +1.5 °C, end-century: +2.5 °C), and relative humidity increases are pronounced (up to 4%) in the Northwestern region (Fig. S2), mean HI values remain similar to SSP1-2.6. This apparent discrepancy may be due to the HI adjustments explained in the Methodology, and underscores the non-linear interaction between temperature and humidity: mild humidity changes can either enhance or buffer thermal stress, particularly in transitional climate zones such as northwestern India22,68. In DJF and MAM, the mean HI will be under normal levels in most of the country, with negative HI values of 0 to − 27 °C in the Himalayan region and above 27 °C over the Southwestern coast. In JJA the HI will be higher, with HI in the range of 27–32 °C covering most parts of the country. The persistence of negative HI values over the Himalayan region during DJF and MAM further highlights the importance of elevation-driven climatic gradients, where temperature increases may not sufficiently counterbalance lower absolute humidity levels, resulting in comparatively lower thermal stress59,60.

caption.p: Heat-Index mean projections for the SSP2-4.5 scenario for the middle (2041–2070; top row) and far (2071–2100; bottom row) futures.

sec.p: Under the high-emission SSP5-8.5 scenario (Fig. 4), temperature and humidity jointly contribute to pronounced increases in thermal stress by late-century (Fig. S3). A majority of the Indian population is projected to experience frequent “Caution” level HI (27–32 °C) conditions during summer (JJA), reflecting significantly increased health risks and reduced outdoor productivity. By the end of the century, the “Extreme Caution” HI threshold (> 32 °C)—associated with significantly increased heat-related morbidity— and the HI > 41 °C, are projected to be reached in the Northeastern region during summer, a densely populated region with limited current adaptation capacity69,70. During the DJF and MAM seasons, the highest HI values are projected to occur along the Southeastern coast, primarily due to combined high temperature and humidity influxes from the Bay of Bengal, influenced by changing ocean-atmosphere interactions and altered cyclonic activity.

A particularly large increase in both maximum temperature (+ 5.5 °C) and humidity (+ 6%) projected for northwestern India in SSP5-8.5 (Fig. S3) is consistent with robust drying over the adjacent Middle East and increased moisture advection toward the Indo-Gangetic plains48,71. Conversely, a projected 6% decrease in relative humidity in Himalayan regions during winter is consistent with amplified warming at higher altitudes and reduced snow cover altering local humidity conditions, potentially moderating HI despite significant warming62,72.

caption.p: Heat-Index mean projections for the SSP5-8.5 scenario for the middle (2041–2070; top row) and far (2071–2100; bottom row) futures.

sec.title: Projected frequency and duration of extreme heat index events across India under different climate scenarios
sec.p: Since the HI is used to define risk thresholds that justify the issuance of public thermal alerts, it is critical to examine not only the frequency but also the persistence (duration) of days exceeding critical HI values. This section thus analyses projected mean changes in seasonal days and duration of HI surpassing 27 °C (“Caution”) and 32 °C (“Extreme caution”) thresholds across India under the three climate scenarios considered. Those thresholds were chosen also taking into account the results obtained for the ensemble mean (Figs. S10–S12), since they are the most common HI thresholds in India.

Figure 5 shows the annual mean number of days (maximum of 92 days per season and year), under the SSP1-2.6 scenario, with a heat index above 27 (HI27+, first and third row) and 32 (HI32+, second and fourth row) each season. Considering that the ‘Caution’ level is exceeded by 5 to 10 annual days in some areas of the north of the country in MAM, and from 20 to 50 in JJA in the historical period (Fig. S5), it is seen that a modest warming (Fig. S1) leads to regionally differentiated increases in heat-stress days. For instance, during winter (DJF), coastal areas experience more than 30 HI27 + days per year, reflecting mild increases in humidity and temperature during cooler months (Fig. S1), influenced by warmer Indian Ocean surface temperatures and increased moisture transport into land62,73. Pre-monsoon months (MAM) see a widespread increase with most regions feeling 50–75 HI27 + days per year with event durations reaching 75 consecutive days (Fig. S7), meaning that this value will be surpassed constantly in the future. During monsoon (JJA) HI27 + days become more extensive surpassing 75 days annually particularly in northern India, where rising temperatures combined with sustained high humidity magnify health risks39,63.

Extreme caution conditions (HI32+) do not exist during DJF, in MAM are concentrated on the coast (lower than 20 days per year), and reach to 75 days per year in the northern India during JJA reflecting the heightened vulnerability in regions exposed to persistent high moisture influx and reduced evaporative cooling potential33,68. The HI results are very similar in both periods, mid and late centuries.

caption.p: Seasonal (DJF, MAM and JJA) CMIP6 GCMs ensemble mean of the annual mean number of HI27 + and HI32 + days in the middle (2041–2070) and far (2071–2100) futures for the SSP1-2.6 scenario.

sec.p: The intermediate scenario, SSP2-4.5 (Fig. 6), highlights a spatial expansion of the areas facing prolonged heat stress events. Coastal heat stress observed under SSP1-2.6 extends further inland during DJF, attributable to increased land-sea thermal gradients and moisture advection towards interior India, altering the spatial extent of humid heat episodes74,75. By the end of the century, HI32 + days become more frequent (10–20) in the Monsoon (JJA) season. The maximum duration of HI32 + events during MAM and JJA also increases notably across the entire country, shifting from brief events (0–1 days) to sustained episodes lasting 5–10 days (Fig. S8). This increased persistence is particularly critical from a health perspective, as prolonged heat exposure exacerbates physiological strain and increases morbidity and mortality risks69,70.

caption.p: Ensemble mean for HI27 + days and HI32 + days in the middle (2041–2070) and far (2071–2100) futures for the SSP2-4.5 scenario.

sec.p: For the high-emission SSP5-8.5 scenario, significant warming intensifies heat-stress impacts dramatically, with frequency and persistence of critical HI thresholds increasing considerably. The extension and the annual numbers of days of HI27 + and HI32 + are projected to double and triple, respectively, compared to historical (1985–2014) baseline (Fig. S5). A significant increase of up to 75 annual days of HI27 + is also observed further south. Notably, in DJF more than 20 days per year of HI27 + appear in the South, and the pre-monsoon (MAM) reveals widespread increases of 50–75 HI27 + days, reflecting earlier onset and prolonged pre-monsoon hot conditions. During monsoon (JJA) most of the country experiences 75–90 days of HI27 + indicating the normalization of heat extremes under unchecked warming conditions. In addition, annual days exceeding the “Extreme caution” threshold (HI32+) are projected to increase more at the end of the century, with 20 to 50 days per year in MAM, and 75 to 92 in JJA (Fig. 7). The persistence (Fig. S9) is higher in the south in MAM (from 50 to 75 days/year) and in the north in JJA (up to 90 days/year). Southern India’s increased extreme HI values persistence in DJF reflects substantial warming coupled with stable humidity conditions, while northern India’s summer persistence (JJA) intensifies due to reduced cooling from decreased monsoon rainfall frequency and increased dry spell durations63,76.

caption.p: Ensemble mean for HI27 + days and HI32 + days in the middle (2041–2070) and far (2071–2100) futures for the SSP5-8.5 scenario.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: In the present study, we examined seasonal maximum temperature anomalies and changes in the heat index (HI) across India during both historical (1951–2020) based on ERA5 reanalysis data, and projected future periods (2015–2100), using CMIP6 model simulations, under three different climate scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5). Given the recognized limitations of CMIP6 climate models, such as differences in spatial resolution, parameterization schemes and biases42, it is essential to assess the accuracy of CMIP6 models against observational datasets before evaluating future climate extremes. Thus, the quality of CMIP6 models was first evaluated by comparing their ability to capture observed warming signals from ERA5 reanalysis and meteorological observations.

The results revealed that CMIP6 ensemble simulations robustly reproduce ERA5-derived reanalysis temperature data during the boreal winter (DJF) and pre-monsoon (MAM), for both mean and extreme temperature values, reflecting a sound representation of large-scale circulation patterns and associated temperature anomalies in cooler seasons. However, during the boreal summer monsoon season (JJA), the CMIP6 ensemble systematically underestimated the warming signal by approximately 50% (0.2σ) as indicated by the standardized temperature anomaly distributions. These findings align with3,35, who also highlighted the similar systematic model biases and limitations of CMIP6 GCMs in representing temperature extremes, particularly their tendency to underestimate the frequency of hot events and overestimate the occurrence of cold events. Such model biases in monsoon seasons are likely due to inadequate representation of convection, moisture transport mechanisms from the Indian Ocean, and local land–atmosphere interactions in coarse-resolution global models77.

Consistent with previous observational analysis31,78,79, this study confirms significant warming trends across the Indian subcontinent since 1980, particularly highlighting larger winter warming rates compared to those obtained in pre-monsoon and monsoon periods. Previous studies with observations reported significant variability in observed seasonal warming rates across India, with winter months showing stronger warming in northern and Himalayan regions due to feedback processes related to declining snow cover, earlier snowmelt, and weakened cold-air intrusions31,80. This physical understanding reinforces confidence in the projected winter warming signals, but suggests caution when interpreting projected summer temperature extremes due to model biases.

Previous studies have demonstrated that the response of heat stress to climate change can be complex and nonlinear12,15,81,82. In general, climate warming can result in an increase in heat-related environmental stress and a decrease in cold-related stress3. This relation is not linear, as it depends on multiple variables. Different combinations of climatic variables (e.g. radiation, wind speed, maximum temperature, etc.), can lead to two opposite conclusions for both normal and extreme conditions19. However, it has been seen that uncertainties in health-related metrics combining temperature and humidity are considerably smaller than if uncertainties in the two variables were independent83.

The employed HI definition depends on the objective of the study. Thus, some studies use the mean temperature at 2 m84 while others use the maximum temperature20 or the temperature of the previous day85 to measure the response to time exposure. The proposed analysis allows us to measure the response of the HI to different climate scenarios, to perform seasonal comparisons and to study the spatial distribution from a climatological perspective. Also, since we are using gridded climate data instead of meteorological observations, the HI27 + and HI32 + thresholds are used here as relative indicators to facilitate comparison across regions and time, rather than as absolute measures of health risk.

The historical JJA HI results (Fig. S4) are comparable to those observed in India for the daily mean UTCI climatology for July, calculated from the ERA5 reanalysis results (Figure 2 of86. The lowest HI values are obtained for the Southwestern region (22 °C), contrary to the larger warming expected on the western coast79, and the highest in the North (27–30 °C). Small HI values could be related to the cool bias seen along the west coast35. Future scenarios results show that the HI will increase in a warmer climate due to the widespread increase in temperature and relative humidity, and will decrease in those areas where humidity decreases. For the end of the century, under the most severe scenario (SSP5-8.5), the large temperature rise will produce an increase of the HI also in the northern Himalayan region, even with a decrease in humidity. These results are in line with the projected rise in the frequency, persistence and extension of heatwaves35,36 and also with previous works over the USA87 and Vietnam88. In the mid-century, the maximum HI will increase to more than 10 days per year in those countries and more than a four-fold increase from historical conditions is expected at the end of the century under the most extreme scenario. Here, it should be noted that HI results for summer may be overestimated, as observed in the comparison of CMIP6 maximum temperatures with observations. To address this overestimation of the models, a bias correction of the simulations is often performed. However, it should be noted that univariate bias correction ignores dependencies between variables and may introduce inconsistencies in the derived variables89. In addition, bias correction methods are often based on historical periods, assuming that biases are stationary over time90. Yet, biases in GCMs may evolve under climate change, particularly for extreme heat events that are projected to intensify in India, which could compromise the robustness of bias correction91. Consequently, bias correction may dampen the projected climate change signal.

The analysis of events above critical HI values reflects that the averaged number of extremes will increase annually. In the most severe scenario, SSP5-8.5, the annual days with HI27 + will increase from 5 to 10 to 20–50 days/year in DJF, depending on the area, and will be more than 50 days/year in the whole of India in MAM. Regarding the health impact, the increase in the persistence of such events is even more important. The longest event is projected to increase as much as the average number per season. It is important to take into account that the mean HI is analysed here, and the result values would be larger if the maximum temperatures for the maximum HI were used. The impact of this result on society will depend on the growth of the population in the most exposed areas19,82,87.

The national-scale approach allows consistent comparison of signals across India, clearly capturing key spatial contrasts (coastal, Indo-Gangetic plain, and Himalayan regions) without the added uncertainty that subdividing the data at 1.25° resolution could introduce. The results emphasize the spatial and temporal variations in the duration and occurrence of extreme events across different regions of India. This agrees with previous studies about extreme events in India38,92. These findings have significant implications for climate change adaptation and mitigation strategies, indicating that different regions may face varying levels of heat stress under different climate change scenarios. Future research should incorporate higher-resolution data or urban-scale modelling to better capture local heat stress conditions and population exposure, particularly in densely populated cities. This is especially relevant as the majority of India’s population remains in rural areas, where gridded mean data provide a more representative picture of background climatic stress than urban station records.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This work explores the decadal evolution of the climate change signal in India in past and future periods. The historical analysis is made using the ERA5 reanalysis data, and the future assessment is performed with the CMIP6 GCMs, in agreement with three emission scenarios (SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5). The datasets were regridded to a common regular grid of 100 km, allowing for the study of the spatial characteristics of global warming at the regional scale. Furthermore, the impact of climate change on heat stress is studied by calculating the heat index (HI). The most relevant findings can be summarized as follows:

list-item.p: Regardless of the season, there has been an increase in the climate change signal in India, this increase was larger in the most recent decade (2011–2020), and greater in DJF than in the other seasons. The high resolution of ERA5 and the CMIP6 GCMs allows seeing the differences in the standardized temperature anomaly curves among seasons. This is the case of the extreme cold and hot frequencies. In DJF and JJA there has been a decrease in the cold events and an increase in the hot extremes, which have duplicated in the last decade, while in MAM the mean or ’normal’ climate is more extreme with time, both in the cold and hot events.

The CMIP6 GCMs have revealed good capacity at representing the climate change signal, as obtained with ERA5 reanalysis in winter (DJF). However, CMIP6 models tend to overestimate India’s warming in both the summer (JJA) and the pre-monsoon (MAM) seasons. Those biases are important to take into account when using future climate projections over India with confidence and be able to interpret their results.

Overall, the projected climate change signal for the 21st century is stronger for winter than for the other seasons, independently of the scenarios considered (SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5 and the SSP5-85). In the most severe scenario (SSP5-85), the increase in extremely anomalous events will become normal frequencies in the future. Spatially, temperature increases the most in the North of India and expands to the southern regions throughout the 21st century. This is accompanied by a widespread rise in relative humidity in most Indian regions.

Changes in the local climate to which the human body is adapted have an impact on heat stress. A warmer and more humid climate is related to higher heat stress. The HI will increase with the rise in temperatures and humidity, and will stabilize in those scenarios that take mitigation measures into account. The largest HI values are seen during the summer in the Northeast part of India, and the lowest in the South and the Himalayan regions, due to the decrease in humidity, increasing to the north with time and emission scenario. In terms of public health, the four-fold increment in the longest duration of the events with HI27 + at the end of the century could have major implications.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: Below is the link to the electronic supplementary material.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work is supported by the Portuguese Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia, FCT, I.P./MCTES through national funds (PIDDAC): UID/50019/2023 , LA/P/0068/2020 e UID/50019/2025 https://doi.org/10.54499/LA/P/0068/2020) and https://doi.org /10.54499/UID/PRR/50019/2025. M.O. Molina was supported by the Portuguese Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (FCT) through the DRI/India/0098/2020 project (https://doi.org/10.54499/DRI/India/0098/2020), the Instituto Dom Luiz through the UID/PRR/50019/2025 (https://doi.org/10.54499/UID/PRR/50019/2025), and by the Horizon Europe research and innovation programs under grant agreement no. 101081661 (WorldTrans). AA extends thanks to the Anusandhan National Research Foundation for the research grant (CRG/2023/003449) facilitated at IIT Roorkee.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: M.O. Molina contributed in the conceptualization, methodology, formal analysis, writing and discussion.PM Soares and R. Trigo contributed in the conceptualization, methodology, writing and discussion.AA contributed in the methodology, writing and discussion.

notes.title: Funding
notes.p: This work is supported by the Portuguese Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia, FCT, I.P./MCTES through national funds (PIDDAC): UID/50019/2023, LA/P/0068/2020 e UID/50019/2025 10.54499/LA/P/0068/2020) and 10.54499/UID/PRR/50019/2025. M.O. Molina was supported by the Portuguese Fundação para a Ciência e a Tecnologia (FCT) through the DRI/India/0098/2020 project (10.54499/DRI/India/0098/2020), the Instituto Dom Luiz through the UID/PRR/50019/2025 (10.54499/UID/PRR/50019/2025), and by the Horizon Europe research and innovation programs under grant agreement no. 101081661 (WorldTrans). AA extends thanks to the Anusandhan National Research Foundation for the research grant (CRG/2023/003449) facilitated at IIT Roorkee.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data cannot be made publicly available upon publication because no suitable repository exists for hosting data in this field of study. The data that support the findings of this study are available upon reasonable request from M.O. Molina (mosanchez@ciencias.ulisboa.pt).

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
